query: current evidence on functional tests as biomarkers in Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Tests
retrieved_docs.content: The serum enzymes, especially CPK, are increased to more than 10 times normal, even in infancy and before the onset of weakness. [150] Diagnosis is suggested (a high CPK level does not confirm the diagnosis because many other alterations can also increase CPK) by measuring the blood CPK level, which can be 100 times the normal level, with diagnostic confirmation by genetic testing for mutations in the dystrophin gene. [151] Serum CPK levels are invariably elevated between 20 and 100 times normal in Duchenne muscular dystrophy. [152] The levels are abnormal at birth, but values decline late in the disease because of inactivity and loss of muscle mass. [153] Elevated CPK levels at birth are diagnostic indicators of Duchenne muscular dystrophy. [154] Identifying female carriers of the condition is not achievable with certainty, but serum CPK is elevated in 60% to 80% of carriers. [155] Serum CPK can be 2 to 20 times above normal in Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy. [156]

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Evaluation -- Other Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Chromosomal analysis: The DNA deletion size does not predict clinical severity in Becker and Duchenne dystrophies. [161] In approximately 95% of Becker dystrophy cases, "in-frame" mutations, despite the DNA deletion, maintain the translational read frame of messenger RNA. This preservation enables the development of dystrophin, as evident in the presence of dystrophin on Western blot examination. [162] Electrocardiogram: Often, patients will have annual electrocardiograms (ECGs) as a proactive measure to monitor the potential development of cardiomyopathy. This diagnostic study reveals atrial and atrioventricular rhythm disturbances. The typical ECG findings include an increased net RS in lead V1, deep, narrow Q waves in the precordial leads, and A QRS complex too narrow to be right bundle branch block; and tall right precordial R waves in V1. [163] [164] The dominant R wave in lead V1 is a crucial clue for an accurate diagnosis. [165]

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Evaluation -- Other Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Electromyography (EMG) reveals characteristic features of myopathy during the assessment. Enables evaluation of muscle denervation, myopathies, myotonic dystrophy, and motor neuron disease. EMG demonstrates features typical of myopathy. [170] In clinical examination and EMG, changes are detectable in nearly any muscle, marked by the waxing and waning of potentials termed the "dive bomber effect." [171] Genetic testing: A definitive diagnosis of muscular dystrophy can be established through mutation analysis performed on peripheral blood leukocytes. [172] Genetic testing reveals deletions or duplications of the dystrophin gene in 65% of patients with Becker dystrophy, a proportion similar to Duchenne dystrophy. [173] Multiplex polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is commonly employed to detect deletions, while sequencing is utilized to identify other mutations. [174]

retrieved_docs.title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A dystrophinopathy should be suspected in patients with symptoms of weakness, characteristic physical exam, and a possible family history of the disease. Laboratory testing involves creatine kinase measurements, muscle biopsies, gene testing, and ECG findings for cardiomyopathy. [7] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Evaluation -- Serum Creatine Kinase (CK)
retrieved_docs.content: Serum CK measurements are elevated before the development of clinical symptoms and signs and may also be elevated in newborns. Levels peak by age two and can be more than 10 to 20 times above the upper limit of normal. As age and disease progress, serum CK levels decrease as fibrosis and fat progressively replace muscle. Other muscle enzymes, such as aldolase levels and AST levels, may also elevate. Asymptomatic carriers may also have elevated CK levels. This is seen in about 80% of cases, and the highest levels are noted between ages 8 and 12.

retrieved_docs.title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Evaluation -- Gene Analysis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with DMD demonstrate the complete or near-complete absence of the dystrophin gene. Dystrophin immunoblotting can be used to predict the severity of the disease. In DMD, patients are found to have less than 5% of the normal quantity of dystrophin.

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Duchenne muscular dystrophy
retrieved_docs.content: Genetics: X-linked recessive disorder caused by mutations in the DMD gene. Onset: Typically early childhood (around 3 to 5). Clinical features: Progressive muscle weakness, particularly in the pelvic and shoulder girdle muscles. Diagnostic tests: Elevated CK levels, genetic testing for DMD gene mutations. Prognosis: Rapid progression, wheelchair dependence by adolescence, cardiopulmonary complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Serum Myoglobin -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In addition, serum myoglobin levels have been used to evaluate neuromuscular diseases, such as muscular dystrophy, muscular atrophy, and polymyositis. [66] Percy et al reported that combining creatine kinase, hemopexin, and myoglobin measurements significantly enhanced the detection of Duchenne muscular dystrophy carriers compared to using creatine kinase and hemopexin alone or any of these tests individually. [67]

retrieved_docs.title: Dystrophinopathies -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Creatine phosphokinase (CK) is the best initial test to perform. Values to consider DMD are above 10-times normal limits, while for BMD are above 5-times the normal limit. DCM CK values could present in a wide range above normal reference. Other tests that may present with high values are aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH).

retrieved_docs.title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Differential Diagnosis -- Intermediate form of Muscular Dystrophy
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with this form of dystrophy have dystrophin levels between DMD and BMD.

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation therapy (RT) has been studied as a potential treatment for muscular dystrophy, particularly in the context of Duchenne muscular dystrophy.

retrieved_docs.title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Differential Diagnosis -- Beckers Muscular Dystrophy (BMD)
retrieved_docs.content: BMD has a later onset, and the length of survival is longer. Patients typically have higher concentrations of dystrophin protein.

retrieved_docs.title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Evaluation -- Electromyography
retrieved_docs.content: Characteristic myopathic features can be seen; however, this is nonspecific. Motor and sensory nerve conduction velocities are normal, and denervation is not present.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lactate Dehydrogenase -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Increased LDH-5 in serum is a marker for muscular dystrophy.

retrieved_docs.title: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Western blot: Duchenne dystrophy can be diagnosed through Western blot analysis of muscle biopsy specimens, revealing abnormalities in dystrophin proteins' quantity and molecular weight. In Becker muscular dystrophy, dystrophin levels may appear normal on Western blot despite the abnormal protein, contrasting with significantly decreased dystrophin levels in individuals with Duchenne muscular dystrophy. [185]

retrieved_docs.content: under the term penicilamin, indicating a sub‑category of thiol‑containing chelating agents used in rheumatology. Beyond antirheumatic agents, several drugs are listed as members of “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system in ATC.” These include Palovarotene, a retinoic‑acid‑receptor agonist used for skeletal disorders; aceneuramic acid, a substrate‑replacement therapy for certain metabolic myopathies; and eteplirsen, an antisense oligonucleotide employed in the treatment of Duchenne muscular dystrophy. Their inclusion underscores the diversity of pharmacologic options available for musculoskeletal conditions, ranging from disease‑modifying agents to gene‑targeted therapies. Overall, the hierarchy connects anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic products to the musculoskeletal drug class, situates specific antirheumatic agents (such as gold preparations, quinolines, and penicillamine derivatives) within that framework, and places additional musculoskeletal therapies like

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 821
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the knowledge network depicts a hierarchical and interrelated system in which exercise‑based therapies (المعالجة بالتمارين) serve as a foundational parent for various specialized modalities, including ultrasound‑based interventions (المعالجة بموجات فوق صوتية) and broader rehabilitative approaches (المعالجة التأهيلية). These modalities are applied across disorder‑specific therapies—such as connective tissue disorder therapy and musculoskeletal disorder therapy—demonstrating an integrated therapeutic paradigm that leverages both physical exercise and advanced energy‑based treatments to optimize patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1622
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] serves as a parent category that includes both nedokromil and nusinersen. Nedokromil is identified as a member of the class of antiallergic inhalants for obstructive airway diseases, excluding corticosteroids, indicating its use in respiratory conditions where allergy‑mediated bronchoconstriction is a concern. Nusinersen, while also belonging to the broader N [Preparations] group, is a distinct therapeutic agent used for the treatment of spinal muscular atrophy, reflecting a neuromuscular application rather than a respiratory one. This organization demonstrates that the N [Preparations] category encompasses a range of medications with different mechanisms and clinical indications, from inhaled antiallergic agents like nedokromil to antisense oligonucleotide therapies such as nusinersen.

Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of clinical presentations (symptoms) and the underlying, often vague, disease or injury processes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation when managing patients with ill‑defined conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 569
retrieved_docs.content: Aceneuramic acid, eteplirsen, and nusinersen are all classified within the ATC group “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system,” indicating that each is used to address conditions affecting the musculo‑skeletal system. Nusinersen’s therapeutic action is characterized by an increase in protein synthesis, a physiologic effect that contributes to its clinical benefit in these disorders. The shared classification of these three agents underscores their common relevance to musculo‑skeletal disease management, while the distinct mechanism of increased protein synthesis by nusinersen highlights the importance of understanding individual drug actions for optimal patient care.
